overview: Mono-White Initiative leverages fast mana and low-cost attackers to establish immediate board presence in Vintage. By attacking early and often, the deck triggers growth and card draw abilities to outpace opponents before they can stabilize. The strategy relies on maintaining relentless pressure while efficiently cycling through resources.
win_conditions: Overwhelming the opponent with a large, growing White Plume Adventurer
Generating card advantage through repeated Seasoned Dungeoneer attacks
Closing out games quickly before opponent's late-game answers resolve

keep_criteria: Hands containing Chrome Mox and at least one one-drop creature
Hands that can attack by turn one
Keep any hand with Chrome Mox plus at least one land and one haste creature.
Also keep hands with two lands and two haste creatures if they include a removal spell or combat trick.
Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 Chrome Mox or a turn-1/2 haste creature.
mulligan_criteria: Hands with zero artifacts or creatures
Hands stuck on three or more lands
Mulligan hands with three or more lands, no Chrome Mox, and no early threats, or hands with only one land and no acceleration.

early_game: Play Chrome Mox immediately to accelerate mana
Drop White Plume Adventurer and Seasoned Dungeoneer on curve
Attack every turn to trigger abilities and establish pressure
Turn 1: Play Chrome Mox, tap it for one white mana, and play a basic land.
If you have a haste creature, play it on turn 1 if you can afford it (1W), otherwise hold for turn 2.
Turn 2: Attack with haste creatures, trigger death/draw effects, and use the card advantage to play more threats or answers.
Prioritize playing lands to ensure you can cast your spells on curve.
Sequence Chrome Mox early to unlock turn-2 plays, but remember it won't untap until your next turn.
mid_game: Prioritize attacking over blocking to maximize triggers
Use drawn cards to maintain board presence and apply pressure
Look for ways to grow Adventurer into an unblockable threat
Use Chrome Mox to maintain mana advantage, but be mindful of the one-turn delay for untapping.
Prioritize keeping cards in hand to leverage death triggers and maintain pressure.
Manage your graveyard carefully if the opponent has graveyard hate, and avoid overcommitting to the board without card advantage.
Commit threats when you have card advantage or when the opponent has no removal.
Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to preserve resources for a specific interaction.
Use removal to protect your board or remove key threats, and prioritize trading creatures when it generates favorable card advantage.
late_game: Commit to all-out attacks to close the game quickly
Avoid stalling, as Vintage control decks stabilize over time
Use card advantage to out-resource opponents in attrition
Unblocked haste creatures, combat tricks, and direct damage spells.
Use card advantage to generate multiple threats that are difficult to block or remove.
Leverage the +1/+1 counters from White Plume Adventurer to close the game in one or two turns.
Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no removal or sweepers, or when you can close the game in one or two turns.
Commit when you've forced the opponent to use their removal on less important spells, leaving your key threats unanswerable.

key_cards.name: White Plume Adventurer
key_cards.role: Primary aggressive threat that grows each turn
key_cards.notes: Attack with it every turn to add +1/+1 counters, quickly making it difficult to block and a major damage source.

key_cards.name: Chrome Mox
key_cards.notes: Play on turn one to enable early creature drops and ensure you can attack on curve without mana screw.

key_cards.name: Seasoned Dungeoneer
key_cards.role: Card advantage engine and early pressure
key_cards.notes: Attack with it to draw cards, refilling your hand to maintain tempo and find answers or additional threats.

sequencing_tips: Always attack with Seasoned Dungeoneer before blocking to draw cards
Play Chrome Mox before lands to optimize early turns
Attack with White Plume Adventurer even if it dies, as the counter triggers on attack declaration
Prioritize damage over board control in the early game
Avoid overcommitting to the board without card advantage.
Be mindful of the one-turn delay on Chrome Mox and plan your mana accordingly.
Track the opponent's graveyard and hand to anticipate threats.
Practice sequencing to maximize death triggers and card advantage.
Remember that Mono-White Initiative thrives on tempo and card advantage, so prioritize plays that maintain pressure while drawing into more resources.

matchups.advice: Attack relentlessly to prevent them from setting up counterspells and board wipes.
Disrupt their setup by attacking early and forcing them to use interaction on your creatures.
Use Seasoned Dungeoneer to draw into removal or larger threats, and trade efficiently.

Use cheap threats, artifact spells, and multiple threats to overwhelm counterspells. Sequence plays to force counters on less important spells, and use Chrome Mox to cast spells at instant speed when possible. Consider bringing in artifact hate and additional pressure if counterspells are prevalent.

Apply early pressure to race their ramp. Use fast mana to stay ahead and use removal to disrupt their mana sources. Attack around their ramp spells and use combat tricks to close the game before they can cast their late-game threats.

Win by leveraging card advantage, tracking removal, and out-sequence your opponent. Use sideboard tricks and removal to gain an edge. Prioritize trading creatures when it generates favorable card advantage, and be mindful of the opponent's hand to avoid walking into targeted removal.

common_threats: Watch for counterspells, removal, and sweepers in the opponent's hand.
Pay attention to their mana base and graveyard to anticipate their strategy.
Track their life total and board state to know when to pressure or stabilize.
Note when they tap out for a key spell, as this is often your window to attack or cast a combat trick.
